nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich lagoons and seaward reefs at depths of about 1–15 m; juveniles tend to form schools in shallow water.

diet.notes: Primarily a benthic-algae grazer. Has been documented eating hair algae, Valonia (bubble algae), red turf algae, macroalgae, and microalgae. In aquariums, dried algae sheets (nori), spirulina-based flake or pellet, and herbivore-formulated frozen foods are generally recommended; mysis, krill, and brine shrimp may be accepted as occasional supplements. Frequent feeding is commonly advised to discourage coral nipping.

goodWith: most peaceful and semi-aggressive reef community fish
avoidWith: predatory species large enough to consume it

imageCandidates.notes: Leonard Low; 2624x1728. Tack-sharp, vibrant full-body side view showing both dark oblique forebody bars, blue-and-yellow scribbled lines and yellow tail. Best diagnostic and quality match.
Rickard Zerpe; 1722x1292. Clean in-situ side profile, whole fish in frame, clear eye bar and behind-head bar, blue pencil-streaks and yellow tail. Slightly softer background.
T. Cooper; 1000x665. Striking sharp pair over staghorn coral showing natural pairing and full coloration; minor ReefLifeSurvey watermark along the bottom edge (not over the fish).

summary: A peaceful Western Pacific herbivore with two dark bars across the forebody and blue pencil-streak markings. Generally considered reef-safe with caution; well-fed specimens tend to leave corals alone. Has venomous dorsal, anal, and pelvic spines that should be handled with care.
careNotes:
The Two Barred Rabbitfish is generally regarded as a hardy, moderately demanding saltwater fish suited to established systems with ample swimming room and natural algae growth.

Temperature: Aquarium sources commonly recommend 22–26 °C (72–78 °F), though wild populations have been observed across a wider preferred range of roughly 25.5–29.3 °C.

Venomous spines: All dorsal and anal fin spines, along with the leading pelvic spines, are venomous and can inflict a painful sting. The toxin is heat-labile, meaning heat may help break it down if a sting occurs. Handling should always be done with a net or aquarium tools — never with bare hands.

Reef safety: Generally considered reef-safe with caution. Well-fed specimens tend to ignore corals, but underfed fish have been known to nip at soft corals, LPS (large-polyp stony corals), tunicates, and sponges. Frequent feeding of algae-based foods is strongly advised in mixed reef aquariums.

Conspecific aggression: Adults have been known to be aggressive toward other rabbitfish (family Siganidae), so only one should generally be kept per system unless housing a confirmed mated pair in a very large tank. Juveniles tend to school in shallow water in the wild.

Color camouflage: When stressed, sleeping, or startled, the fish may shift into a mottled dark color pattern. This is normal defensive coloration and not a sign of illness.

Trade name note: The common name 'Two Barred Rabbitfish' is shared in the aquarium trade with Siganus virgatus (the Double Barred Rabbitfish or Barhead Spinefoot), and some retailer listings use the names interchangeably or apply them to the wrong species. Siganus doliatus is also sold under the names Scribbled Rabbitfish, Barred Spinefoot, and Blue-lined Rabbitfish. Captive lifespan has not been clearly documented; related Siganus species are commonly reported to live 5–10 years.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented reliably in home aquariums. Like other Siganidae, the species is a pelagic broadcast spawner that responds to lunar and tidal cues, making replication in home systems essentially infeasible without specialized facilities.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Original collection URL (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish) returned only summary data; this product page is the dedicated LiveAquaria listing for 'Two Barred Rabbitfish (Barred Spinefoot)'. Note: LiveAquaria appears to have a long-standing taxonomic error and lists the scientific name as 'Siganus virgatus' on this page, but the common-name listing 'Barred Spinefoot' and the trade context (search-result aggregation) indicate the listing actually covers Siganus doliatus. Verified S. doliatus identity against FishBase, BlueZoo Aquatics, Quality Marine, FreshMarine, and ReefApp.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Siganus doliatus, adult size ~10 in, native range Western Pacific/Fiji, reef-safe with caution, intermediate care, peaceful temperament. Minimum tank cited as 60 gal — the lowest among consulted sources; averaged with larger figures and biased to 150 gal (568 L) per Stage C tank rule.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Siganidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 25 cm SL, trophic level 2.0 (herbivore), depth 0–15 m, native distribution (Palau and Kosrae north to NW Australia and Tonga; eastern Indonesia), preferred environmental temperature 25.5–29.3°C (wider than trade 22–26°C — averaged to 22–27°C). IUCN: Least Concern (2015).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Siganus doliatus, ~10 in max, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 150 gal minimum, intermediate care, herbivore, peaceful (aggressive to conspecifics), reef-safe with caution. Notes the camouflage color-change behavior.

additional.name: FreshMarine (Scribbled Rabbitfish)
additional.notes: Confirmed Siganus doliatus, up to ~11 in, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 100 gal (also cites 75 gal elsewhere), moderate-but-venomous care, herbivore, peaceful except with rabbitfish, reef-safe with caution.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked max length 25 cm, family Siganidae, native distribution (Australia, Indonesia, central/west Pacific), reef-safe with caution, peaceful but aggressive to conspecifics. Cites minimum tank 800 L (211 gal) — the highest among consulted sources; the spread of 60–211 gal across sources is wide but a 150 gal / 568 L pick sits between the trade average and ReefApp's specialist figure.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner responding to lunar and tidal cues; captive breeding has not been documented reliably in home aquariums.
